real_passage: World War I was a pivotal moment in the twentieth century in terms of reinventing societal roles. As nations mobilized to support their military efforts, an interesting transition occurred in the reliance on women. Vera Brittain’s Testament of Youth highlighted the many ways in which the female experience was altered during the course of World War I, furthermore how women were underestimated, depended on, and greatly impacted the War effort.Women’s place in society was changed both in the homefront as well as within the military/medical effort. Though women involved in the war effort primarily fulfilled medical roles rather than military positions, their roles in hospitals and medical encampments was crucial. Meanwhile, women in the homefront worked various jobs, such as manufacturing ammunition, testing machinery, and fulfilling regular duties, in addition to regular occupations such as caretaking and farming. Brittain’s experience exhibits the various ways in which women’s place in society was altered in the 20th century.

real_passage: Karel Capek’s 1936 novel War with the Newts is a captivating science fiction story which examines European politics and worldwide social commentary. Karel Capek, a Czech author born in 1890, also wrote Rossum’s Universal Robots in 1920, a play that explores sentient robots and their effect on society. Overall, his works are mostly science fiction that echo many political themes of his era, some dealing with subjects that have yet to occur. Many of the topics and ideas discussed in War with the Newts predates the Second World War, but is eerily similar to events that unfold. The plot itself follows the discovery and eventual exploitation of a Newt population, and explores their interaction with human society in various parts of the world. The book declines to follow one specific protagonist or character, which I find amusing. Instead, the book is separated into three sections. The first section deals with the discovery of a newt-like species living near an island off the coast of Sumatra.

real_passage: The article from USA Today offers a deep and personal look into the lives of individuals in Michigan who have battled with eating disorders, sharing their unique experiences, challenges, and paths toward recovery. It presents a collection of stories that highlight not only the struggles and dangers associated with eating disorders but also the hope and possibility for healing. The individuals' stories roughly cover the broad spectrum of symptoms associated with eating disorders, including restrictive eating, binge eating, purging, and excessive exercise (although this is something we didn’t really cover in class). These symptoms align with clinical descriptions of disorders like anorexia nervosa, bulimia nervosa, and binge-eating disorder, showcasing a wide array of behaviors and physical health consequences, from malnutrition to the psychological toll of these conditions. The article points to societal pressures, like the glorification of thinness and the influence of social media, as well as personal and psychological factors, including trauma, the need for control, and issues of self-worth. The diversity of these factors in the stories told emphasizes that eating disorders do not have a single cause but rather emerge from a complex interplay of individual, environmental, and societal influences. In terms of treatment, the article vividly illustrates the idea that recovery from eating disorders is a multifaceted and non-linear process that includes therapy, nutritional counseling, and sometimes medical intervention.

real_passage: Undergraduate roommate relationships can play a role in shaping students' academic and social experiences. Traditionally, roommates can be assigned randomly or made via existing friendships, but the prevalence of social media has turned it into a space for potential roommate seeking. It is becoming more and more common to see Facebook groups and Instagram pages with incoming freshmen students introducing themselves and seeking potential roommates and friends. The nature of these roommate relationships, whether formed online or assigned randomly, can significantly impact stress levels, personal health, and academic performance (Dusselier et al., 2005), suggesting that the method of roommate pairing might be as influential as the quality of the relationship itself. The present study explores the methods of roommate pairing, especially the effects of meeting on social media, while also taking into account the total amount of interpersonal interactions between the roommates in terms of the quantity of conflict resolution discussions. The study by Eisenberg et al. (2014) provides some understanding of peer effects within university environments, particularly focusing on how students' behaviors are influenced by their roommates. Participants took a survey upon move-in and another near the end of the first year. The study found notable peer effects on binge drinking, while there was less evidence for peer effects on other activities such as gambling. Having a roommate who engaged in binge drinking increased the likelihood that a student would also engage in binge drinking.

real_passage: Respondents will engage with a series of structured questionnaires designed to assess the perceived strength of friendship and the level of concordance with their roommates. After the initial survey determines roommate assignment method, the remaining eligible participants will take another survey upon move-in to determine baseline data. At the end of the semester, before winter break, the respondents will be surveyed again. To measure the frequency of conflict resolution, we adapted a conflict resolution scale previously validated in peer relationship studies, called the Conflicts and Problem Solving Scales (Kerig, 1995). Because the CPS was originally designed by Kerig to measure four dimensions of conflict between couples (frequency, severity, resolution, and efficacy), we altered it to prioritize frequency, measured as a rating of the number of times roommates engage in both minor and major conflicts in a month, set on a 0 (never) to 7 (multiple days a week) scale. See Appendix A. In addition, respondents will answer questions based on aspects of efficacy as well, with items such as “We can openly discuss our differences,” concerning the potential suppression of conflict resolution discussions, and “When we have a disagreement, we usually come to a mutually agreeable solution,” both measured using a 0 (strongly disagree) to 5 (strongly agree) Likert scale.
